revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Gōzoku]][[fa:گوزوکو]][[ff:Gōzoku]][[fr:Gōzoku]][[it:Gōzoku]][[ja:豪族]][[pt:Gōzoku]][[vi:Gōzoku]][[zh:豪族 (日本)]]
{{Short description|Japanese term for powerful and wealthy families}} {{Italic title|reason=
}} [[File:Mononobe no Arakabi.jpg|thumb|{{ill|Mononobe no Arakabi|en|Mononobe no Arakabi|qid=Q1114837|lt=Mononobe no Arakabi|1=Mononobe no Arakabi}}, a government minister and ''gōzoku'' of the Yamato period]] {{nihongo|'''''Gōzoku'''''|豪族|extra={{lit.}} "powerful [[Japanese clans|clan]]"}}, in {{ill|Japanese language|en|Japanese language|qid=Q5287|lt=Japanese|1=Japanese language}}, refers to powerful regional families. In historical context, it can refer to powerful non-royal families regardless of their area of influence, in contrast to the {{ill|Imperial House of Japan|en|Imperial House of Japan|qid=Q909452|lt=Imperial Family|1=Imperial House of Japan}}.<ref name=":0">{{Cite book |url=https://www.worldcat.org/oclc/14970117 |title=Nihon dai hyakka zensho |others=Shōgakkan, 小学館 |year=2001 |isbn=4-09-526001-7 |edition=Shohan |at=豪族 |oclc=14970117}}</ref> The most powerful ''gōzoku'' families of the {{ill|Yamato period|en|Yamato period|qid=Q633881|lt=Yamato period|1=Yamato period}} included the [[Soga clan]], [[Mononobe clan]] and [[Katsuragi clan]].
== History == In the {{ill|Yamato period|en|Yamato period|qid=Q633881|lt=Yamato period|1=Yamato period}} prior to the [[Ritsuryō|''ritsuryō'' system]], the powerful clans based in {{ill|Yamato Province|en|Yamato Province|qid=Q907495|lt=Yamato Province|1=Yamato Province}} and {{ill|Kawachi Province|en|Kawachi Province|qid=Q1047150|lt=Kawachi Province|1=Kawachi Province}} were referred to as the ''chūō gōzoku'' (central ''gōzoku''), while the term ''chihō'' ''gōzoku'' (regional ''gōzoku'') referred to powerful clans outside the immediate vicinity of the capital who served as ''[[kuni no miyatsuko]]'' and ''[[agatanushi]]''. The central ''gōzoku'' held ''[[kabane]]'' titles such as ''[[Omi (title)|omi]]'' and ''[[muraji]]'', while the regional ''gōzoku'' held titles such as ''atai'' and ''[[Kimi (kabane)|kimi]]''.<ref name=":0" /> The most powerful ''daigōzoku'' ("great ''gōzoku''") included the [[Katsuragi clan]], [[Ōtomo clan (ancient)|Ōtomo clan]], [[Mononobe clan]], and [[Soga clan]].<ref name=":1">{{Cite book |title=Obunsha Nihonshi jiten ed. 3 旺文社日本史事典 三訂版 |publisher=Obunsha 旺文社 |year=2000 |isbn=9784010353134 |at=豪族}}</ref>
After the implementation of the ''ritsuryō'' system, the central ''gōzoku'' became ''kizoku'', the aristocracy, and took part in central politics. The regional ''gōzoku'' remained practically unchanged and took part in regional politics.<ref name=":1" /> Under the ''ritsuryō'' system, ''gōzoku'' referred to powerful regional families holding a [[List of Japanese court Ranks, positions and hereditary titles|court Rank]] of Sixth Rank or below or who were unranked, in contrast to the royals and the aristocracy, who held a court Rank of Fifth Rank or above. Under this system, the ''gōzoku'' typically served as district governors or as junior officials in provincial governments, and thus took on the duties of local administration.<ref name=":0" />
In the Middle Ages, ''gōzoku'' referred to a class of regional officials such as ''{{ill|Jitō|en|Jitō|qid=Q1329805|lt=Jitō|1=Jitō}}'' (territory governor), ''shōkan'' (manor administrator) and ''geshi'' (junior official at a manor). However, after {{ill|Muromachi period|en|Muromachi period|qid=Q334845|lt=Muromachi period|1=Muromachi period}}, the term ''gōzoku'' fell out of use and was replaced by the term ''dogō'', which referred to ''{{ill|Jizamurai|en|Jizamurai|qid=Q13432011|lt=Jizamurai|1=Jizamurai}}'' (provincial peasant-warrior in charge of a small rural domain), ''kumon'' (official at a manor in charge of official documents) and ''satanin'' (official at a manor in charge of executing orders and judgements on behalf of the lord).<ref name=":0" />
During and after the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|qid=Q184963|lt=Edo period|1=Edo period}}, neither the terms ''gōzoku'' nor ''dogō'' were used. The terms ''{{ill|gōnō|en|gōnō|qid=Q11634393|lt=gōnō|1=Gōnō}}'' and ''gōshō'' replaced the previous class distinction following the rise of wealthy farmers and wealthy merchants during the Edo period.<ref name=":0" />
== See also ==
* {{ill|gōnō|en|gōnō|qid=Q11634393|lt=gōnō|1=Gōnō}} * [[Nanushi]]
